title: Heidegger: Being and Time and the Care for the Self - ResearchGate
text:
Being and Time does not develop a philosophical theory among others, rather it faces the challenge of thoroughly reflecting upon the dilemma that is constantly present in philosophy, namely the question of human being and its relation to being in general. From this point of view, I would like to consider the possibility of reading this fundamental work of Heidegger as an ethics of the care, that is, as book that promotes a cultivation of the self and the other. camino (pp. 3-31). Barranquilla: Uninorte. Watson, P. (2000). A terrible beauty. A history of the people and ideas
that shaped the modern mind. New York: Weidenfels & Nicholson. Wolin, R. (1990). The politics of being: The political thought of Martin

... The cultivation of self is not an explicit topic in Heidegger's works, though some scholars suggest that Being and Time could also be interpreted as a book about self-care. Jesús A.
Escudero (2013) supports this view, citing Heidegger's assertion that "man's perfection -his transformation into that which he can be in Being-free for his own most possibilities (projection) -is 'accomplished' by 'care'" (Heidegger, 1962, p. 243). Escudero posits that Being and Time can be read as "an ethics of care, that is, as a book that promotes a cultivation of the self and the other" (2013, p. 302).

In this sense, Karl Löwith shrewdly portrays the peculiar
personality of the young Heidegger: “A Jesuit by education, he
became a Protestant through indignation; a dogmatic through
education; an existential pragma tist through experience; a theo-
logian by tradition, and an athe ist as researcher” (Löwith, 1986:
p. 45). Different aspects of a person who attempts to grasp a
phenomenon as mysterious, as slippe ry and as foggy as that of
human existence in its utter factic ity. Life presents itself to the
young Heidegger as an enigma waiting to be un derstood. The
decipherment of that enigma sets the course for an early phi-
losophical itinerary that will gloriously culminate in his great
book, Being and Time (1927). Heidegger responds to this problem from a totally new per-
spective: an analysis of human life and its peculiar ability to
face its inherent tendency to fall.
